title: On the moral status of attention you cannot give back
summary: Argue that drawing someone's sustained attention — in education, media, or relationships — carries a moral obligation, because attention is a finite and non-renewable resource.
topic: The moral weight of claiming and using other people's attention
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay on the ethics of taking or holding another person's attention. Consider at least one domain — teaching, journalism, social media, or a long-term relationship — where the obligation becomes concrete. Use inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'granted… even so…' concession. Avoid the language of self-help productivity culture. Approach the question as a moral one, not a personal efficiency one.
hintStarters: Rarely is the taking of attention treated as a moral act — yet it may be among the most consequential…
What distinguishes a legitimate claim on attention from an illegitimate one is not duration but…
Granted, every act of communication requires an audience. Even so,…

hintVocab.meaningVi: có tính chất khai thác, chiếm đoạt
băng thông chú ý, khả năng chú ý có hạn
mối quan hệ giả xã hội, quan hệ một chiều với người nổi tiếng
khán giả bị giam cầm, người buộc phải chú ý
sự bất cân xứng về ảnh hưởng
tính cân xứng, sự tương xứng
vai trò quản lý, trông coi
sự cấp bách được tạo ra giả tạo
có tính chất uỷ thác, người được tin tưởng

sampleText:
Rarely is the taking of attention treated as a moral act — yet it may be among the most consequential things one person does to another. We have elaborate frameworks for the ethics of taking money, time, or physical space; we have almost none for the ethics of taking the twenty minutes of focused thought that a person might otherwise have given to their child, their work, or themselves. Attention, unlike money, cannot be returned. Once spent, it is simply gone.

The irreversibility is what gives the question its moral weight. When a newspaper editor chooses to run a story above the fold, when a teacher designs a unit of work, when a platform engineer adjusts an algorithm to maximise session time — each is making a claim on cognitive labour that the audience cannot reclaim. This is not merely a technical decision. It is a moral one, and the asymmetry of influence between the person who designs the claim and the person who pays it makes the moral weight fall disproportionately on the designer.

The sharpest case is the teacher, because the audience is captive and the power differential is explicit. A teacher who fills forty minutes with poorly prepared material, or with content that serves their own comfort rather than the students' development, has not merely wasted time — they have consumed a non-renewable resource without adequate justification. It is the fiduciary character of the teaching relationship, not merely its contractual character, that makes this a matter of professional ethics rather than simple incompetence. The student cannot opt out; their attention is conscripted.

What distinguishes a legitimate claim on attention from an illegitimate one is not duration but proportionality — the relationship between what is asked and what is genuinely offered in return. A two-hour lecture that changes how a student understands a field is proportionate; a two-hour lecture that rehearses material the student already has at greater cost than it would take to read it is not. The same logic applies to journalism. A long investigation that exposes systemic harm earns the attention it claims; a manufactured controversy — a story whose urgency is artificially inflated to sustain engagement — extracts attentional bandwidth without delivering commensurate understanding.

Granted, every act of communication requires an audience, and every claim on attention involves a unilateral act: the writer writes before knowing who will read, the teacher prepares before knowing who will attend. Even so, the unilateral character of the claim does not dissolve the obligation; if anything, it intensifies it. Precisely because the recipient of the claim cannot negotiate in advance, the person making it carries a greater responsibility to exercise the kind of stewardship that a negotiated exchange would impose from outside.

The proliferation of platforms engineered to maximise time-on-screen has made this ethics newly urgent. What social media discovered — and what the engagement bait deployed across news, entertainment, and education now exploits — is that manufactured urgency and the promise of deferred reward can hold attention more reliably than genuine value can. The person who spends three hours moving between notifications has not freely chosen that expenditure in any meaningful sense; they have had their attentional bandwidth extracted through mechanisms designed to bypass their deliberate judgement. The moral responsibility for that extraction does not sit with the individual user.

It is the absence of reciprocity that marks the extractive claim most clearly. In a healthy epistemic relationship — a good lecture, a careful piece of journalism, a sustained conversation — the claim on attention is accompanied by a genuine return: knowledge, orientation, connection. In an extractive one, the return is manufactured: the sensation of engagement without its substance, the feeling of being informed without the information. What one owes to those whose attention one claims is not merely accuracy or interest; it is the genuine effort to return something worth the price.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after a negative adverb for moral gravitas
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Rarely is the taking of attention treated as a moral act' đảo động từ 'is' trước chủ ngữ sau 'Rarely'. Cấu trúc đảo ngữ này tạo hiệu ứng trang trọng, nhấn mạnh tính ngoại lệ của luận điểm trung tâm ngay từ câu mở.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Rarely is the taking of attention treated as a moral act' inverts the copula 'is' before its subject after negative adverb 'Rarely' — a C1 inversion that opens with formal weight, framing the essay's central claim as a neglected moral reality.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to foreground the key differentiator
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the fiduciary character of the teaching relationship, not merely its contractual character, that makes this a matter of professional ethics rather than simple incompetence.' dùng cấu trúc 'It is X that Y' để đặt trọng tâm vào X — tính chất uỷ thác — đồng thời loại trừ ngay một cách diễn giải khác (tính hợp đồng) bằng 'not merely'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the fiduciary character of the teaching relationship, not merely its contractual character, that makes this a matter of professional ethics…' uses 'It is X that Y' to foreground the fiduciary dimension while simultaneously rejecting the contractual framing with 'not merely' — a precise C1 move combining cleft focus with embedded concession.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hiếm khi việc chiếm đoạt sự chú ý được coi là một hành động đạo đức — nhưng đó có thể là một trong những điều hệ trọng nhất mà một người làm với người khác. Chúng ta có các khuôn khổ phức tạp cho đạo đức của việc lấy tiền, thời gian, hay không gian vật lý; nhưng hầu như không có gì cho đạo đức của việc lấy hai mươi phút suy nghĩ tập trung mà một người đáng lẽ có thể đã dành cho con cái, công việc, hay bản thân họ. Sự chú ý, không giống tiền bạc, không thể hoàn trả. Một khi đã tiêu, nó đơn giản là biến mất.

Tính không thể đảo ngược là điều mang lại trọng lượng đạo đức cho câu hỏi này. Khi một biên tập viên báo chọn đăng một tin trên trang nhất, khi một giáo viên thiết kế một đơn vị bài học, khi một kỹ sư nền tảng điều chỉnh thuật toán để tối đa hoá thời gian phiên — mỗi người đang đưa ra yêu cầu về lao động nhận thức mà khán giả không thể đòi lại. Đây không chỉ là quyết định kỹ thuật. Đó là một quyết định đạo đức.

Trường hợp rõ ràng nhất là giáo viên, vì khán giả bị giam cầm và sự chênh lệch quyền lực là hiển nhiên. Chính tính chất uỷ thác của mối quan hệ dạy học, không chỉ tính hợp đồng của nó, mới làm cho điều này trở thành vấn đề đạo đức nghề nghiệp.

Điều phân biệt yêu cầu chính đáng đối với sự chú ý với yêu cầu không chính đáng không phải là thời lượng mà là tính cân xứng. Cứ cho là mọi hành động giao tiếp đều cần có người tiếp nhận, và mọi yêu cầu về sự chú ý đều liên quan đến một hành động đơn phương. Dù vậy, tính chất đơn phương của yêu cầu không xoá bỏ nghĩa vụ; nếu có, nó còn tăng thêm.

Sự vắng mặt của tính có đi có lại là điều đánh dấu yêu cầu khai thác rõ ràng nhất. Trong một mối quan hệ nhận thức lành mạnh, yêu cầu về sự chú ý đi kèm với một sự đáp trả thực sự. Điều người ta nợ những người có sự chú ý mà mình yêu cầu không chỉ là sự chính xác hay thú vị; đó là nỗ lực thực sự để trả lại điều gì đó xứng đáng với cái giá phải trả.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly applies inversion after a negative adverb?
options: Rarely the taking of attention is treated as a moral act.
Rarely is the taking of attention treated as a moral act.
The taking of attention rarely is treated morally.
Rarely, attention taking is not treated as moral.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Rarely' at the start, the copula 'is' must precede the subject — 'Rarely is the taking of attention treated…'

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay say attention has greater moral weight than money or time?
options: Because attention is more difficult to measure than money
Because unlike money, attention cannot be returned once spent
Because people value attention more than financial resources
Because attention is the only resource that affects cognition
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states explicitly: 'Attention, unlike money, cannot be returned. Once spent, it is simply gone.' — its irreversibility is the moral key.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the contrast between attention and money.

mcQuestions.prompt: What makes the teacher case the 'sharpest' according to the essay?
options: Teachers are paid specifically to manage attention
The audience is captive and the power differential is explicit
Teaching involves longer sessions than other forms of attention claim
Students are more vulnerable than adult audiences
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says 'the sharpest case is the teacher, because the audience is captive and the power differential is explicit.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for what makes the classroom a special moral situation.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'proportionality' mean as a standard for legitimate attention claims?
options: The duration of the claim should match the length of the relationship
The ratio of what is asked to what is genuinely offered in return
The number of people whose attention is claimed at once
The financial value of the content delivered
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines proportionality as 'the relationship between what is asked and what is genuinely offered in return'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the definition in the paragraph beginning 'What distinguishes a legitimate claim…'

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what does 'manufactured urgency' describe in media and social platforms?
options: Breaking news that genuinely requires immediate public response
Artificially inflated urgency designed to sustain engagement without delivering commensurate value
A journalistic technique for structuring a news story
The pressure journalists feel to publish quickly in competitive markets
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes 'a manufactured controversy — a story whose urgency is artificially inflated to sustain engagement — extracts attentional bandwidth without delivering commensurate understanding.'

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay say the unilateral character of a communication claim intensifies rather than dissolves the moral obligation?
options: Because the sender is legally liable for the content they transmit
Because the recipient cannot negotiate in advance, so the sender must exercise self-imposed stewardship
Because unilateral communication always reaches a larger audience
Because it is harder to measure the impact of a unilateral claim
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues: 'Precisely because the recipient of the claim cannot negotiate in advance, the person making it carries a greater responsibility to exercise the kind of stewardship that a negotiated exchange would impose from outside.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for what 'cannot negotiate in advance' implies for the sender.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft to isolate a key moral dimension?
options: The fiduciary character of the teaching relationship makes this a matter of professional ethics.
It is the fiduciary character of the teaching relationship, not merely its contractual character, that makes this a matter of professional ethics.
What makes teaching ethical is its fiduciary character rather than its contractual character.
Teaching's moral weight derives from its fiduciary and contractual characters combined.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not merely Y, that Z' is a cleft that foregrounds X and simultaneously rejects Y as insufficient — combining focus with embedded concession.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is … that …' structure.

options: A professional relationship between colleagues who have never met in person
A one-sided relationship in which one person invests emotional attention in a public figure who does not know them
A harmful relationship that damages one person's social standing
A therapeutic relationship between patient and counsellor
mcQuestions.explanation: Parasocial relationships are defined by their asymmetry: one party (an audience member) invests attention and affect in a public figure who has no reciprocal awareness of them.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about the 'para-' prefix meaning 'alongside' or 'resembling but not quite'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'the sensation of engagement without its substance'?
options: The physical feeling of using a touchscreen device
The impression of meaningful interaction or learning without the actual understanding that genuine engagement produces
The enjoyment of content that does not lead to any practical outcome
The anxiety people feel when they disengage from social media
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay contrasts genuine epistemic return with a manufactured simulation of it: feeling engaged without actually acquiring knowledge or connection.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the parallel construction '…without its substance, the feeling of being informed without the information'.

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay claims moral responsibility for extracted attention 'does not sit with the individual user'. Who does it sit with?
options: The government regulators who permitted the platform to operate
The advertisers who fund the platform
The platform engineers and designers who built mechanisms to bypass deliberate judgement
The parents of young users who allowed access to the platform
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'The moral responsibility for that extraction does not sit with the individual user' — implicitly placing it on those who designed the extractive mechanisms.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at who is described as engineering the maximisation of attention.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition correctly signals a concession followed by a counter-argument?
options: Even so, communication requires an audience. Granted, this is unilateral.
Granted, every act of communication requires an audience. Even so, the unilateral character of the claim does not dissolve the obligation.
Furthermore, communication needs an audience; in addition, this is unilateral.
Although communication is unilateral, granted this dissolves obligation.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted… Even so…' signals concession then pivot; the order must be: acknowledge the opposing point first, then reassert.
mcQuestions.hint: The concession ('Granted') always precedes the pivot ('Even so').

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'epistemic duty' imply in the context of attention claims?
options: The duty to verify facts before publishing them
A knowledge-related obligation — specifically, the responsibility to genuinely inform or advance understanding when claiming attention
The legal duty of a journalist to protect sources
The academic obligation to cite all sources used
mcQuestions.explanation: Epistemic duty, in this context, is the obligation to deliver genuine understanding — not merely the appearance of it — to those whose attention you have claimed.
mcQuestions.hint: The 'epistemic' relates to knowledge — what you owe in terms of genuine understanding.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which best paraphrases 'extractive claim on attention'?
options: A formal request made in a legal document
Demanding attention while returning little or nothing of genuine value
A research methodology that gathers data from human participants
A polite but persistent communication style
mcQuestions.explanation: Extractive implies taking without adequate return — the essay contrasts this with claims accompanied by 'genuine return: knowledge, orientation, connection'.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'extractive' means in the context of natural resources.

options: Bandwidth is measurable in minutes while time is not
Attentional bandwidth captures the cognitive capacity to focus, not just the clock duration of attention
Bandwidth refers only to digital media consumption
Attentional bandwidth is always renewable unlike time
mcQuestions.explanation: Attentional bandwidth refers to the capacity for focused cognitive engagement — which is finite in a richer sense than mere time, because it can be depleted even when clock time remains.
mcQuestions.hint: Consider what 'bandwidth' suggests about limited processing capacity.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'deferred reward' describe in the context of social media engineering?
options: Payment systems that delay financial compensation
The promise of a satisfying outcome (a like, a reply, a revelation) that keeps a user engaged in anticipation
A teaching strategy that delays assessment until the end of a unit
Journalism that investigates long-term consequences rather than immediate events
mcQuestions.explanation: Deferred reward is a behavioural mechanism — the intermittent promise of a future satisfying outcome keeps users scrolling or clicking in anticipation, holding their attention beyond any genuine value.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what keeps someone reading or scrolling even when nothing satisfying has appeared yet.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a time when someone claimed your sustained attention — in a class, a meeting, or through media — and either honoured or failed to honour that claim. What made the difference? What do you think the person or institution owed you, and did they deliver it?
